summary: Term begins (20 Jan); returns to ‘Iran cannot have a nuclear weapon’; low direct signal.
Reimposes maximum pressure; ‘drive oil exports to zero’; ‘all options on the table.’
Letter to Khamenei with a ~60-day deadline; warns a military option ‘would be a terrible thing.’
First indirect talks (Oman/Rome) ‘constructive’; floats meeting Khamenei.
Hardens on ‘zero enrichment’ red line; ‘serious consequences’ warning; claims a deal is ‘close.’
Orders strikes on Fordow/Natanz/Isfahan (21–22 Jun); ‘obliterated’; then declares a ceasefire (24 Jun).
‘Peace through strength’; aggressive oil-sanctions enforcement (~115 entities, 30 Jul); talks dormant.
Continued pressure; backs the E3 snapback while leaving the door open.
Low-signal month; pressure-plus-openness as snapback mechanics dominate.
‘Ready to reach an agreement with Iran’ (13 Oct); optimistic tone.
Low-signal; the diplomatic opening lingers without a framework.
Reverts to ‘zero enrichment’/full dismantlement as Iran’s unrest grows.
Cancels meetings amid the protests; ‘help is on its way’; surges naval forces.
10-day ultimatum (20 Feb); launches Operation Epic Fury (28 Feb); talks suspended.
Peak war rhetoric: ‘UNCONDITIONAL SURRENDER’; infrastructure threats; brief oil-sanctions easing (~20 Mar).
Coercive diplomacy: short ceasefires, failed Islamabad talks, then a naval blockade (13 Apr).
‘Negotiating on fumes’; claims a settlement is ‘largely negotiated’ while running ~25 May ‘defensive’ strikes.
Backed a negotiated settlement amid Trump’s max-pressure; UK ‘focused on supporting the political process.’
Urged ‘restraint, calm and a return to diplomacy’; declined to join the US strikes.
UK (with France/Germany) triggered the UN snapback (28 Aug), citing Iran’s ‘significant non-performance.’
UN sanctions reimposed; UK added measures on 70+ Iranian individuals/entities (29 Sep).
Joined the 9 Jan E3 statement condemning the protest crackdown; moved toward IRGC sanctions.
After Epic Fury, ‘not involved’ but UK planes ‘in the sky’ for a defensive op; condemned Iran’s attacks.
UK announced ‘defensive military action’ (1 Mar); joined E3 condemnation of Iran’s ‘indiscriminate’ attacks.
Welcomed the 8 Apr ceasefire; priorities: reopen Hormuz and curb the nuclear/missile programmes.
Condemned Iran’s strikes on UAE oil infrastructure; urged meaningful negotiations.
Welcomed the 24 Jun ceasefire; ‘dialogue, diplomacy and de-escalation.’
Expelled Iran’s ambassador (26 Aug), suspended the Tehran embassy, pledged to list the IRGC.
Listed the IRGC as a state sponsor of terrorism (27 Nov); Wong: ‘acts of aggression… on Australian soil.’
Fresh sanctions over the protest crackdown (‘horrific use of violence’).
28 Feb joint statement: ‘Australia stands with the brave people of Iran’; Iran ‘a destabilising force.’
Backed the US/Israel effort but ruled out ground troops; deployed defensive assets to the UAE; demanded clarity on objectives.
Canada broadened Iran sanctions-listing criteria (6 Mar) across the handover.
Hosted G7 Kananaskis (16 Jun): Iran ‘principal source of regional instability’; urged ‘maximum restraint.’
Backed the snapback; implemented the reimposed UN sanctions (30 Sep).
More sanctions (14 Feb); 28 Feb backed the US ‘acting to prevent Iran obtaining a nuclear weapon.’
Walked back support ‘with regret’ (3 Mar) as ‘inconsistent with international law’; ‘Canada… will never participate.’
Co-signed the 8 Apr ceasefire statement; would support reopening the Strait of Hormuz.
Lobbied against US–Iran talks; threatened unilateral strikes; Trump warned him off.
Launched Operation Rising Lion (13 Jun); post-ceasefire claimed a ‘historic victory.’
Pressed Trump for a joint decapitation strike; Israel’s side of Epic Fury = ‘Roaring Lion.’
Sustained the joint air campaign; ground forces entered Lebanon after Hezbollah joined.
Welcomed the ceasefire but ‘not the end… a stop on the way’ to all objectives.
Wary acceptance of the deal track rather than new strikes (interpretive).
Reluctantly allowed indirect talks after Trump’s letter.
‘We are not seeking war, or nuclear weapons. We seek dialogue.’
pezeshkian_note: No Supreme Leader statement this month; block reflects President Pezeshkian.

summary: Dismissed US ‘control’ demands as ‘fantasy’; no surrender of enrichment.
Defiant post-ceasefire address (26 Jun); the pre-warned Al Udeid strike framed as calibrated retaliation.
Ruled out talks and an enrichment halt as snapback hit; called it ‘illegal.’
pezeshkian_note: Pezeshkian struck a softer tone at UNGA — ‘never sought a nuclear bomb’ (Cautious).

summary: Told Trump to ‘keep dreaming’ that the strikes destroyed the program.
Ordered a violent crackdown on the protests (casualty figures wildly disputed ⚠).
Killed 28 Feb in Epic Fury; Iran fired ~170 missiles in retaliation.
Installed ~8 Mar (59/88); first statement (12 Mar, read out — he did not appear) vowed revenge and to keep Hormuz closed.
Agreed (8 Apr, Pakistan-mediated) to a ceasefire and to reopen Hormuz; extended 21 Apr.
Negotiating a Hormuz/de-mining framework; Fars insists the strait stays ‘under Iran’s management’ (residual defiance).
Refused Saudi airspace/territory for the attack; condemned Israeli ‘aggressions against the brotherly Islamic Republic of Iran.’
Hosted FM Araghchi in Jeddah for ‘fruitful’ talks.
Authorized force against Iranian incursions but avoided joining the offensive (strategic restraint).
Denied use of Prince Sultan airbase for the US Hormuz escort mission; backed Pakistan diplomacy.
Supported the diplomatic Hormuz track (strong oil-market de-escalation incentive).
Backed US–Iran diplomacy with a hard edge: ‘nuclear experts around the table’ or ‘a more dangerous Iran.’
Call with FM Araghchi; stressed EU readiness to engage on the nuclear file.
‘Iran must not be allowed to develop a nuclear weapon’; urged all sides to step back; joined Geneva talks.
EU follows the UN snapback in reimposing sanctions; ‘must not be the end of diplomacy.’
EU condemned the crackdown; FAC (29 Jan) agreed to list the IRGC as terrorist + new measures.
Condemned Iran’s retaliation but distanced the EU: ‘This is not Europe’s war… We were not consulted.’
Welcomed the 8 Apr ceasefire as ‘a step back from the brink’; ‘the Strait of Hormuz must be open.’
Focused on Hormuz freedom of navigation (EU Aspides mission needs ‘more ships’).
